subject: Physics
chapter: Wave Motion and Waves on a String

questions.subject: Physics
questions.chapter: Wave Motion and Waves on a String
questions.q: A sine wave propagates through a medium. What is the minimum distance between two particles that always possess the same speed?
questions.solution:
Let the equation of the propagating sine wave be $y = A \sin(\omega t - kx)$.

The velocity of a particle at position $x$ and time $t$ is given by:

$v = \dfrac{\partial y}{\partial t} = A \omega \cos(\omega t - kx)$

The speed of the particle is the magnitude of its velocity:

$|v| = A \omega |\cos(\omega t - kx)|$

For two particles at positions $x_1$ and $x_2$ to always possess the same speed, their speeds must be equal for all values of time $t$:

$|\cos(\omega t - kx_1)| = |\cos(\omega t - kx_2)|$

This condition implies:

$\omega t - kx_1 = \pm (\omega t - kx_2) + n\pi$

Since this must hold true for all time $t$, the coefficient of $t$ on both sides must be the same. Thus, we must take the positive sign:

$\omega t - kx_1 = \omega t - kx_2 + n\pi$

$k(x_2 - x_1) = n\pi$

Substituting $k = \dfrac{2\pi}{\lambda}$ and $\Delta x = x_2 - x_1$:

$\dfrac{2\pi}{\lambda} \Delta x = n\pi$

$\Delta x = \dfrac{n\lambda}{2}$

To find the minimum distance between two distinct particles, we take $n = 1$:

$\Delta x = \dfrac{\lambda}{2}$

questions.subject: Physics
questions.chapter: Wave Motion and Waves on a String
questions.q: The equation $y = A \sin^2(kx - \omega t)$ characterizes a wave motion with
options: does not characterize a wave motion
questions.solution:
Given equation of the wave is $y = A \sin^2(kx - \omega t)$

Using the trigonometric identity $\sin^2 \theta = \dfrac{1 - \cos 2\theta}{2}$, we get:

$y = \dfrac{A}{2} [1 - \cos(2kx - 2\omega t)]$

$y = \dfrac{A}{2} - \dfrac{A}{2} \cos(2kx - 2\omega t)$

This represents a wave superimposed on a constant displacement of $\dfrac{A}{2}$.

The amplitude of the wave is the magnitude of the coefficient of the cosine term, which is $\dfrac{A}{2}$.

The angular frequency of the wave is $\omega' = 2\omega$.

The frequency of the wave is $f = \dfrac{\omega'}{2\pi} = \dfrac{2\omega}{2\pi} = \dfrac{\omega}{\pi}$.

questions.subject: Physics
questions.chapter: Wave Motion and Waves on a String
questions.q: Identify the mechanical wave from the options below.
questions.solution:
Mechanical waves require a material medium for their propagation.

Sound waves require a medium (solid, liquid, or gas) to travel and cannot propagate through a vacuum. Hence, they are mechanical waves.

Radio waves, light waves, and X-rays are electromagnetic waves, which do not require a material medium for propagation.

questions.subject: Physics
questions.chapter: Wave Motion and Waves on a String
questions.q: Among the following equations, which one describes a wave propagating along the $Y$-axis?
questions.solution:
A traveling wave is represented by a function where the position and time variables appear together in the phase as $(kx \pm \omega t)$ or $(ky \pm \omega t)$.

Options (1) and (2) represent standing waves because the spatial and temporal variables are separated into a product of trigonometric functions.

Option (3) represents a wave propagating along the $X$-axis because the phase is $(kx - \omega t)$.

Option (4) represents a wave propagating along the $Y$-axis because the phase is $(ky - \omega t)$, with the displacement of particles occurring along the $X$-axis.

questions.subject: Physics
questions.chapter: Wave Motion and Waves on a String
questions.q: A sine wave travels through a medium. At a specific instant, a particular particle exhibits zero displacement. The distance to the closest particle that also has zero displacement is
questions.solution:
The equation of a sine wave travelling through a medium can be represented as $y = A \sin(kx - \omega t + \phi)$.

At a specific instant $t$, the displacement of a particle is zero when the phase is an integral multiple of $\pi$.

Let the position of the first particle be $x_1$. For zero displacement, $kx_1 - \omega t + \phi = n\pi$, where $n$ is an integer.

The closest particle that also has zero displacement will be at a position $x_2$ such that its phase differs by $\pi$.

Therefore, $k(x_2 - x_1) = \pi$.

Since the wave number $k = \dfrac{2\pi}{\lambda}$, we get:

$\dfrac{2\pi}{\lambda} \Delta x = \pi$

$\Delta x = \dfrac{\lambda}{2}$

Thus, the distance to the closest particle with zero displacement is $\lambda/2$.

questions.subject: Physics
questions.chapter: Wave Motion and Waves on a String
questions.q: As a wave generated by a boat passes by, a cork floating in a calm pond undergoes simple harmonic motion with frequency $\nu$. The frequency of the wave is
questions.solution:
When a wave propagates through a medium, the particles of the medium execute simple harmonic motion. The frequency of oscillation of the particles is exactly equal to the frequency of the wave.

Since the cork is floating on the water, it acts like a particle of the medium and oscillates up and down with the same frequency as the wave.

Given that the frequency of the simple harmonic motion of the cork is $\nu$, the frequency of the wave must also be $\nu$.

questions.subject: Physics
questions.chapter: Wave Motion and Waves on a String
questions.q: Two strings $A$ and $B$, constructed from the identical material, are subjected to the same tension. The radius of string $A$ is twice the radius of string $B$. A transverse wave propagates on $A$ with speed $v_A$ and on $B$ with speed $v_B$. What is the ratio $v_A/v_B$?
questions.solution:
The speed of a transverse wave on a string is given by $v = \sqrt{\dfrac{T}{\mu}}$, where $T$ is the tension and $\mu$ is the linear mass density.

The linear mass density can be written as $\mu = \rho \pi r^2$, where $\rho$ is the density of the material and $r$ is the radius of the string.

Since both strings are made of the same material and subjected to the same tension, $T$ and $\rho$ are constant.

Therefore, the speed $v$ is inversely proportional to the radius $r$:

$v \propto \dfrac{1}{r}$

The ratio of the speeds is:

$\dfrac{v_A}{v_B} = \dfrac{r_B}{r_A}$

Given that $r_A = 2r_B$, substituting this value gives:

$\dfrac{v_A}{v_B} = \dfrac{r_B}{2r_B} = \dfrac{1}{2}$

questions.subject: Physics
questions.chapter: Wave Motion and Waves on a String
questions.q: As depicted in the figure, both strings are composed of the same material and share the same cross section. The pulleys are assumed to be light. If the transverse wave speed in string $AB$ is $v_1$ and in string $CD$ is $v_2$, then the ratio $v_1/v_2$ is
questions.solution:
Let the tension in the string $AB$ be $T_1$ and the tension in the string $CD$ be $T_2$.

Since the pulley $B$ is light (massless), the net force acting on it must be zero. The string $AB$ passes around pulley $B$, so there are two upward segments of this string supporting the pulley, each exerting a force $T_1$. The string $CD$ pulls downwards on the axle of the pulley with tension $T_2$.

Balancing the forces on pulley $B$, we get:
$2T_1 = T_2 \Rightarrow \dfrac{T_1}{T_2} = \dfrac{1}{2}$

The speed of a transverse wave in a string is given by $v = \sqrt{\dfrac{T}{\mu}}$, where $T$ is the tension and $\mu$ is the linear mass density.

Since both strings are made of the same material and have the same cross-sectional area, they have the same linear mass density $\mu$.

The ratio of the transverse wave speeds is:
$\dfrac{v_1}{v_2} = \sqrt{\dfrac{T_1 / \mu}{T_2 / \mu}} = \sqrt{\dfrac{T_1}{T_2}}$

Substituting the ratio of tensions:
$\dfrac{v_1}{v_2} = \sqrt{\dfrac{1}{2}} = \dfrac{1}{\sqrt{2}}$

questions.subject: Physics
questions.chapter: Wave Motion and Waves on a String
questions.q: The velocity of sound in air is $332 \text{ m s}^{-1}$. Its velocity in a vacuum will be
questions.solution:
Sound is a mechanical wave which requires a material medium for its propagation.

Since a vacuum contains no particles to transmit the wave, sound cannot travel through it.

Therefore, the concept of the velocity of sound in a vacuum is meaningless.

questions.subject: Physics
questions.chapter: Wave Motion and Waves on a String
questions.q: A wave pulse travelling along a two-piece string experiences partial transmission and partial reflection at the junction. The shape of the reflected wave is inverted relative to the incident wave. Assuming the incident wave possesses a wavelength $\lambda$ and the transmitted wave possesses a wavelength $\lambda'$,
options: the relationship between $\lambda$ and $\lambda'$ cannot be determined
questions.solution:
When a wave pulse reflects from a junction and the reflected wave is inverted, it implies that the wave is reflecting from a denser medium. Thus, the second string is denser than the first string.

Let $\mu_1$ and $\mu_2$ be the linear mass densities of the first and second strings respectively. Since the reflected wave is inverted, $\mu_2 > \mu_1$.

The speed of a transverse wave on a string is given by $v = \sqrt{\dfrac{T}{\mu}}$. Since the tension $T$ is uniform across the two-piece string, the speed of the wave in the second string is less than that in the first string, so $v_2 < v_1$.

The frequency $f$ of the wave remains constant as it passes from one medium to another.

Using the relation $v = f\lambda$, the wavelengths in the two strings are $\lambda = \dfrac{v_1}{f}$ and $\lambda' = \dfrac{v_2}{f}$.

Since $v_2 < v_1$, it follows that $\lambda' < \lambda$.

questions.subject: Physics
questions.chapter: Wave Motion and Waves on a String
questions.q: The resultant wave formed by the superposition of two waves given by $y = a \sin(\omega t - kx)$ and $y = a \cos(\omega t - kx)$ has an amplitude of
questions.solution:
The given equations of the waves are $y_1 = a \sin(\omega t - kx)$ and $y_2 = a \cos(\omega t - kx)$.

The second wave can be written as $y_2 = a \sin\left(\omega t - kx + \dfrac{\pi}{2}\right)$.

The phase difference between the two waves is $\Delta \phi = \dfrac{\pi}{2}$.

The amplitude of the resultant wave is given by $A = \sqrt{a_1^2 + a_2^2 + 2a_1 a_2 \cos(\Delta \phi)}$.

Substituting $a_1 = a$, $a_2 = a$, and $\Delta \phi = \dfrac{\pi}{2}$:

$A = \sqrt{a^2 + a^2 + 2a^2 \cos\left(\dfrac{\pi}{2}\right)}$

$A = \sqrt{2a^2} = \sqrt{2}a$.

questions.subject: Physics
questions.chapter: Wave Motion and Waves on a String
questions.q: Two wires $A$ and $B$, possessing identical geometrical construction, are stretched by a small but equal amount from their natural length. The Young's moduli of the wires are $Y_A$ and $Y_B$ while their densities are $ho_A$ and $ho_B$. It is given that $Y_A > Y_B$ and $ho_A > ho_B$. A transverse signal initiated at one end requires a time $t_1$ to reach the opposite end for wire $A$, and $t_2$ for wire $B$.
options: the information is insufficient to find the relation between $t_1$ and $t_2$
questions.solution:
The speed of a transverse wave on a stretched wire is given by $v = \sqrt{\dfrac{T}{\mu}}$

The tension $T$ in the wire is $T = \dfrac{YA \Delta L}{L}$

The linear mass density $\mu$ is $\mu = \rho A$

Substituting $T$ and $\mu$, we get $v = \sqrt{\dfrac{Y \Delta L}{\rho L}}$

The time taken by the transverse signal to travel the length $L$ is $t = \dfrac{L}{v} = \sqrt{\dfrac{\rho L^3}{Y \Delta L}}$

Since $L$ and $\Delta L$ are identical for both wires, $t \propto \sqrt{\dfrac{\rho}{Y}}$

For wire $A$, $t_1 \propto \sqrt{\dfrac{\rho_A}{Y_A}}$ and for wire $B$, $t_2 \propto \sqrt{\dfrac{\rho_B}{Y_B}}$

Given that $Y_A > Y_B$ and $\rho_A > \rho_B$, both the numerator and the denominator for wire $A$ are greater than those for wire $B$. The ratio $\dfrac{\rho_A}{Y_A}$ can be greater than, equal to, or less than $\dfrac{\rho_B}{Y_B}$.

Therefore, the given information is insufficient to determine the relation between $t_1$ and $t_2$.

questions.subject: Physics
questions.chapter: Wave Motion and Waves on a String
questions.q: Two wave pulses approach each other while travelling in opposite directions on a string. One pulse has a shape that is inverted relative to the other.
options: The pulses will pass through one another, although their shapes will be modified.
The pulses will reflect off one another, such that the rightward pulse will eventually move leftward and vice versa.
The pulses will pass through one another without experiencing any change in their shapes.
The pulses will collide and vanish entirely after the collision.
questions.solution:
According to the principle of superposition, when two wave pulses travelling in opposite directions overlap, the resultant displacement of the string is the algebraic sum of the individual displacements.

The waves do not interact in a way that permanently alters their properties. After the region of overlap, both wave pulses emerge and continue to travel in their original directions with their original shapes, amplitudes, and speeds completely unchanged.

Thus, the pulses pass through one another without experiencing any change in their shapes.

questions.subject: Physics
questions.chapter: Wave Motion and Waves on a String
questions.q: Two periodic waves with amplitudes $A_1$ and $A_2$ travel through a region. Given that $A_1 > A_2$, what is the difference between the maximum and minimum possible resultant amplitudes?
questions.solution:
The resultant amplitude $A$ of two waves with amplitudes $A_1$ and $A_2$ having a phase difference $\phi$ is given by $A = \sqrt{A_1^2 + A_2^2 + 2A_1A_2\cos\phi}$.

The maximum resultant amplitude occurs when $\cos\phi = 1$, which gives $A_{max} = A_1 + A_2$.

The minimum resultant amplitude occurs when $\cos\phi = -1$, which gives $A_{min} = |A_1 - A_2|$. Since $A_1 > A_2$, $A_{min} = A_1 - A_2$.

The difference between the maximum and minimum resultant amplitudes is $A_{max} - A_{min} = (A_1 + A_2) - (A_1 - A_2) = 2A_2$.

questions.subject: Physics
questions.chapter: Wave Motion and Waves on a String
questions.q: Two waves having an equal amplitude $A$ and the same frequency propagate in the same direction within a medium. The amplitude of the resultant wave is
questions.solution:
Let the two waves be $y_1 = A \sin(\omega t - kx)$ and $y_2 = A \sin(\omega t - kx + \phi)$.

By the principle of superposition, the amplitude of the resultant wave is given by $A_R = \sqrt{A_1^2 + A_2^2 + 2A_1A_2\cos\phi}$.

Substituting $A_1 = A_2 = A$, we get $A_R = \sqrt{A^2 + A^2 + 2A^2\cos\phi} = \sqrt{2A^2(1+\cos\phi)}$.

Using the trigonometric identity $1+\cos\phi = 2\cos^2\left(\dfrac{\phi}{2}\right)$, we get $A_R = 2A\left|\cos\left(\dfrac{\phi}{2}\right)\right|$.

Since the phase difference $\phi$ is not specified, it can take any value.

The maximum amplitude occurs when $\cos\left(\dfrac{\phi}{2}\right) = \pm 1$, giving $A_{max} = 2A$.

The minimum amplitude occurs when $\cos\left(\dfrac{\phi}{2}\right) = 0$, giving $A_{min} = 0$.

Thus, the amplitude of the resultant wave lies between $0$ and $2A$.

questions.subject: Physics
questions.chapter: Wave Motion and Waves on a String
questions.q: Two sine waves propagate in the same direction within a medium. Each wave possesses an amplitude of $A$, and the phase difference between them is $120^\circ$. The resultant amplitude is
questions.solution:
The resultant amplitude $A_R$ of two waves with amplitudes $A_1$ and $A_2$ and a phase difference $\phi$ is given by

$A_R = \sqrt{A_1^2 + A_2^2 + 2A_1A_2 \cos \phi}$

Given $A_1 = A$, $A_2 = A$, and $\phi = 120^\circ$.

Substituting these values, we get

$A_R = \sqrt{A^2 + A^2 + 2(A)(A) \cos 120^\circ}$

Since $\cos 120^\circ = -\dfrac{1}{2}$,

$A_R = \sqrt{2A^2 + 2A^2 \left(-\dfrac{1}{2}\right)}$

$A_R = \sqrt{2A^2 - A^2}$

$A_R = \sqrt{A^2} = A$

questions.subject: Physics
questions.chapter: Wave Motion and Waves on a String
questions.q: The fundamental frequency of a vibrating string is directly proportional to the
questions.solution:
The fundamental frequency of a vibrating string is given by the formula:

$f = \dfrac{1}{2L} \sqrt{\dfrac{T}{\mu}}$

where $L$ is the length of the string, $T$ is the tension, and $\mu$ is the linear mass density.

From the formula, it is clear that the fundamental frequency is directly proportional to the inverse of the length of the string, i.e., $f \propto \dfrac{1}{L}$.

The frequency is proportional to the square root of tension ($f \propto \sqrt{T}$), inversely proportional to the square root of density ($f \propto \dfrac{1}{\sqrt{\rho}}$), and inversely proportional to the diameter ($f \propto \dfrac{1}{d}$).

Thus, the fundamental frequency is directly proportional to the inverse of its length.

questions.subject: Physics
questions.chapter: Wave Motion and Waves on a String
questions.q: Consider two waves passing through the same string. The principle of superposition for displacement states that the net displacement of a particle on the string equals the sum of the displacements generated by the two individual waves. Suppose we propose analogous principles for the net velocity and the net kinetic energy of the particle. Such a principle would remain valid for
options: both the velocity and the kinetic energy
the kinetic energy but not the velocity
neither the velocity nor the kinetic energy
the velocity but not the kinetic energy
questions.solution:
According to the principle of superposition, the net displacement of a particle is given by $y = y_1 + y_2$.

The net velocity of the particle is the time derivative of the net displacement:

$v = \dfrac{dy}{dt} = \dfrac{d(y_1 + y_2)}{dt} = \dfrac{dy_1}{dt} + \dfrac{dy_2}{dt} = v_1 + v_2$

Thus, the principle of superposition is valid for the velocity.

The net kinetic energy of the particle is given by:

$K = \dfrac{1}{2}mv^2 = \dfrac{1}{2}m(v_1 + v_2)^2 = \dfrac{1}{2}m(v_1^2 + v_2^2 + 2v_1v_2)$

$K = K_1 + K_2 + mv_1v_2$

Since $K \neq K_1 + K_2$, the principle of superposition is not valid for the kinetic energy.

Therefore, the proposed principle remains valid for the velocity but not the kinetic energy.

questions.subject: Physics
questions.chapter: Wave Motion and Waves on a String
questions.q: A sonometer wire with a natural frequency of $240 \text{ Hz}$ is made to vibrate using a tuning fork of frequency $480 \text{ Hz}$. The frequency at which the wire will vibrate is
questions.solution:
When a sonometer wire is made to vibrate by an external source such as a tuning fork, it undergoes forced vibrations.

In forced vibrations, the system oscillates with the frequency of the applied external periodic force, irrespective of its natural frequency.

Since the frequency of the tuning fork is $480 \text{ Hz}$, the sonometer wire will vibrate with a frequency of $480 \text{ Hz}$.

As $480 \text{ Hz}$ is an integer multiple of the natural frequency ($2 \times 240 \text{ Hz}$), the wire will vibrate in its second harmonic (resonance), but the frequency of vibration will be exactly $480 \text{ Hz}$.

questions.subject: Physics
questions.chapter: Wave Motion and Waves on a String
questions.q: A sonometer wire of length $l$ vibrates in its fundamental mode when excited by a tuning fork having a frequency of $416 \text{ Hz}$. If the length of the wire is doubled while keeping all other parameters the same, the string will
options: vibrate with a frequency of $208 \text{ Hz}$
vibrate with a frequency of $416 \text{ Hz}$
vibrate with a frequency of $832 \text{ Hz}$
questions.solution:
The initial fundamental frequency of the sonometer wire is given by:

$f_1 = \dfrac{v}{2l} = 416 \text{ Hz}$

When the length of the wire is doubled ($l' = 2l$), the new fundamental frequency becomes:

$f_1' = \dfrac{v}{2(2l)} = \dfrac{1}{2} \left(\dfrac{v}{2l}\right) = \dfrac{416}{2} = 208 \text{ Hz}$

The natural frequencies of the new wire are integer multiples of its fundamental frequency, i.e., $208 \text{ Hz}, 416 \text{ Hz}, 624 \text{ Hz}, \dots$

Since the wire is excited by the same tuning fork of frequency $416 \text{ Hz}$, the driving frequency matches the second harmonic of the new wire.

In forced oscillations, a body vibrates with the frequency of the driving source. Because the driving frequency corresponds to one of the natural frequencies of the wire, the wire will resonate in its second harmonic mode (forming two loops) and vibrate with a frequency of $416 \text{ Hz}$.

questions.subject: Physics
questions.chapter: Wave Motion and Waves on a String
questions.q: A sonometer wire carrying a $4 \text{ kg}$ load vibrates in its fundamental mode when excited by a tuning fork of frequency $416 \text{ Hz}$. If the length of the wire between the bridges is subsequently doubled, what must the load be changed to in order to maintain the fundamental mode?
questions.solution:
The fundamental frequency of a sonometer wire is given by $f = \dfrac{1}{2L} \sqrt{\dfrac{T}{\mu}}$, where $L$ is the length, $T$ is the tension, and $\mu$ is the linear mass density.

The tension $T$ is provided by the load $M$, so $T = Mg$.

Since the frequency $f$ and linear mass density $\mu$ remain constant, we have $\dfrac{\sqrt{M}}{L} = \text{constant}$.

Therefore, $\dfrac{\sqrt{M_1}}{L_1} = \dfrac{\sqrt{M_2}}{L_2}$.

Given $M_1 = 4 \text{ kg}$, $L_1 = L$, and $L_2 = 2L$, substituting these values gives:

$\dfrac{\sqrt{4}}{L} = \dfrac{\sqrt{M_2}}{2L}$

$\dfrac{2}{L} = \dfrac{\sqrt{M_2}}{2L}$

$\sqrt{M_2} = 4$

$M_2 = 16 \text{ kg}$

questions.subject: Physics
questions.chapter: Wave Motion and Waves on a String
questions.q: A tuning fork with a frequency of $480 \text{ Hz}$ is employed to vibrate a sonometer wire that has a natural frequency of $410 \text{ Hz}$. The wire will vibrate at a frequency of
questions.solution:
When a tuning fork is used to vibrate a sonometer wire, it sets up forced vibrations in the wire.

In forced vibrations, the system vibrates with the frequency of the external periodic driving force, irrespective of its own natural frequency.

Since the frequency of the tuning fork (the driving force) is $480 \text{ Hz}$, the sonometer wire will vibrate at $480 \text{ Hz}$.
